statusLine: EU fuel reserves remain under strain with 18 of 27 countries holding critical stocks in at least one fuel type despite overall averages above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure as oil prices surge 51.7% since late February, reaching $109.93 per barrel amid Middle East supply disruptions. While EU-wide averages for petrol (113 days), diesel (102.2 days), and jet fuel (96.4 days) remain above the 90-day mandatory minimum, the distribution across member states reveals concerning vulnerabilities.

Romania faces the most severe situation with all three fuel types in critical status, holding just 40.4 days of petrol, 30.2 days of diesel, and 76.4 days of jet fuel. Poland and Luxembourg also show critical levels across all categories. Germany, Europe's largest economy, maintains safe petrol stocks at 100.7 days but holds critically low diesel (64.3 days) and jet fuel (64.7 days) reserves.

The aviation sector appears most vulnerable, with 18 of 27 countries maintaining critical or warning-level jet fuel stocks. This reflects the strategic reserve releases several EU countries have implemented to manage the supply crisis. Diesel stocks show similar strain, with 15 countries below safe levels. Price pressures are intensifying, particularly in Northern European markets where petrol exceeds €2.00 per liter in the Netherlands (€2.341) and Denmark (€2.299).
keyPoints: 18 of 27 EU countries hold critical stock levels in at least one fuel category, despite bloc-wide averages exceeding 90-day minimums
Romania shows critical levels across all fuel types with only 30.2 days of diesel reserves
Jet fuel remains most constrained with 18 countries below safe levels, reflecting aviation sector vulnerability
Germany maintains critically low diesel (64.3 days) and jet fuel (64.7 days) stocks despite safe petrol reserves
Brent crude oil has surged 51.7% since late February to $109.93/barrel, driving pump prices above €2.00/L in Netherlands and Denmark
